coredata.dc:title: Formation mechanism and application potential of carbon dots synthesized from palm kernel shell via microwave assisted method

coredata.dc:description: The present study systematically investigated the influence of synthesis conditions (duration, reaction medium, and doping concentration) and formation mechanism of carbon dots (CDs) derived from low-cost and abundant biomass palm kernel shell (PKS). Surprisingly, the dopant (urea) did not enhance the photoluminescence of CDs as expected, which could be attributed to the low reactivity between the dopant and PKS macromolecules. Variation of synthesis duration from 30 to 120 s clearly indicated the formation mechanism of CDs, involving the stages of dehydration, carbonization, and nucleation. The CDs with the highest photoluminescent intensity and quantum yield was obtained at synthesis duration of 90 s, aligned well with the perfect spherical shape of CDs and the synergistic effects of both surface and carbogenic core conditions. Understanding the formation mechanism could be used to optimize the synthesis of CDs, and hence linked to quantum yield and fluorescent intensity. In terms of application potential, the CDs illuminated well as fluorescent ink and in bacteria cells imaging. The potential of CDs as sensing material has also been proven with the quenching of fluorescence in the presence of metal ions. The linear range for detection of Cu2+ ions was 0.1–0.5 mM with a detection limit as low as 0.05 mM. This signifies the potential of CDs fabricated from PKS as a low-cost and easily available material for Cu2+ ions detection in aqueous solution. The CDs possessed reasonable photo stability as indicated by its consistent fluorescence level even after exposure to UV radiation for a prolonged period of 180 minutes. Overall, a simple, straightforward, and fast method is developed to synthesis strong blue emissive CDs from green PKS that are potentially suitable for Cu2+ ions sensing in real application.

head.ce:title: Formation mechanism and application potential of carbon dots synthesized from palm kernel shell via microwave assisted method

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The present study systematically investigated the influence of synthesis conditions (duration, reaction medium, and doping concentration) and formation mechanism of carbon dots (CDs) derived from low-cost and abundant biomass palm kernel shell (PKS). Surprisingly, the dopant (urea) did not enhance the photoluminescence of CDs as expected, which could be attributed to the low reactivity between the dopant and PKS macromolecules. Variation of synthesis duration from 30 to 120 s clearly indicated the formation mechanism of CDs, involving the stages of dehydration, carbonization, and nucleation. The CDs with the highest photoluminescent intensity and quantum yield was obtained at synthesis duration of 90 s, aligned well with the perfect spherical shape of CDs and the synergistic effects of both surface and carbogenic core conditions. Understanding the formation mechanism could be used to optimize the synthesis of CDs, and hence linked to quantum yield and fluorescent intensity. In terms of application potential, the CDs illuminated well as fluorescent ink and in bacteria cells imaging. The potential of CDs as sensing material has also been proven with the quenching of fluorescence in the presence of metal ions. The linear range for detection of Cu2+ ions was 0.1–0.5 mM with a detection limit as low as 0.05 mM. This signifies the potential of CDs fabricated from PKS as a low-cost and easily available material for Cu2+ ions detection in aqueous solution. The CDs possessed reasonable photo stability as indicated by its consistent fluorescence level even after exposure to UV radiation for a prolonged period of 180 minutes. Overall, a simple, straightforward, and fast method is developed to synthesis strong blue emissive CDs from green PKS that are potentially suitable for Cu2+ ions sensing in real application.

ce:section.ce:para: In this era of globalization, single or multi color emissive luminescent nanomaterials with good biocompatibility have attained great consideration because of their remarkable potential in significant chemical and biological applications. While semiconductor quantum dots (SQD) and organic dyes were greatly used previously as traditional fluorescent dye probes, they do have some drawbacks such as toxicity to humans and the environment and have high photo bleaching [1]. This has led researchers to explore new extraordinary, eco-friendly fluorescent carbonaceous nanomaterials via feasible and green synthesis approach.

Carbon dots (CDs) is a new class of quasi spherical nanoparticles with size less than 10 nm and dominated by sp2 or sp3 hybridized carbon atoms and various functional groups [2]. CDs have attracted the attention of numerous researchers due to their stable luminescence properties, high photo stability, low production cost, high emission quantum yield, long fluorescence lifetime and low cytotoxicity towards biological components [3–5]. At the same time, CDs could also be synthesized from economical feasible, non-toxic, and sustainable carbon sources such as biomass [6–10]. Fluorescent CDs are rising potential candidates to replace traditional dyes and SQD in multidisciplinary fields such as optoelectronic, cellular imaging, drug delivery, fluorescent ink for anti-counterfeiting, photocatalysis, metal cation detection and so on [8,11]. For example, Bcap-37 human breast cancer cell imaging has been studied to explore the potential use of CDs synthesized from albumin, as bioimaging tool with good transmembrane ability [12]. In addition, CDs could be applied as nanosensors to detect small chemical molecules (glucose, phosphate), biological molecules (nucleic acids, protein), and metal cations [13–19]. In the study of D’souza et al. [20], it was proven that the CDs prepared from carrot (carbon precursor) acted as drug delivery tool, which was able to carry the mitomycin drug into MCF-7 cells effectively.

Over the past few years, scientists have been exploring different techniques for CDs synthesis that have made significant differences in the structure, properties and fluorescents mechanisms [8,21]. These techniques are categorized as top-down and bottom-up techniques. The CDs synthesized by top-down techniques are laser irradiation, electrochemical synthesis and chemical oxidation while via bottom-up techniques are microwave irradiation and hydrothermal heating [22]. Recently, microwave irradiation method has appeared as one preferable synthesis approach over other methods due to its simplicity and ability to produce highly luminescent CDs. Microwave irradiation is a fast and inexpensive method for synthesizing CDs through electro-magnetic irradiation with wavelengths ranging from 1 mm to 1 m through a reaction media containing precursor molecules [23]. Many studies successfully synthesized CDs with high quantum yield via microwave irradiation which could be applied in cell imaging, metal detection, fluorescence sensor and so on [24,25]. He et al. [17] proposed a straightforward and easy microwave assisted production of CDs with quantum yield of 51%, which was then applied in biological cell imaging of BT-474 cells. Moreover, in another study, P. Yang et al. [26] have prepared fluorescent CDs from low cost xylan via microwave synthesis with low energy consumption (200 W) within 10 minutes. All of these studies proved that microwave irradiation is an effective, economical, low cost and green CDs synthesis method.

The quality and optical property of CDs (especially the quantum yield and the photoluminescence (PL) intensity are influenced by many synthesis parameters such as pH, temperature, choice of reaction media, types of dopants and carbon precursors, and ratio of carbon source to reaction medium [9,27]. The reaction time could influence the formation of CDs from its precursor, which eventually change the optical properties of the CDs. For instance, Yoshinaga et al. [27] found that the quantum yield of CDs has increased from 0.7% to 3.0% as the hydrothermal duration increased from 30 minutes to 120 minutes. On the other hand, some reports demonstrated that prolonged synthesis duration would destroy the structure of CDs due to uncontrollable carbonization [28]. Whereas, So et al. [24] has reported that no fluorescence was observed from CDs synthesized at microwave irradiation time less than 5 minutes, which concluded that insufficient synthesis duration led to incomplete carbonization of the precursors. Indeed, manipulating the reaction duration could give insight on formation mechanism of the CDs, which is crucial to reveal and understand the specific processes that are involved for complete CDs formation. However, this aspect of study has received less attention and is rarely investigated due to its complicated experimental works which need systematic characteristic measures for identification of specific formation mechanism of CDs [29]. Papaioannou et al. [30] have successfully reported a formation mechanism of CDs by varying the reaction duration from 2 to 12 h, suggesting four formation stages including decomposition of glucose (precursor), followed by aromatization, nucleation and growth of CDs.

In addition, heteroatom doping, surface passivation and functionalization are emerging approaches that researchers have been working on lately to improve the brightness of CDs by adjusting the electronic configuration and energy gap [31]. Typically, CDs prepared from biomass material exhibited weak fluorescence intensity due to unstable surface defects, hence surface passivation or doping is needed to significantly improve the brightness and also the quantum yield [32,33]. For instance, Liao et al. [3] utilized ethylenediamine as doping agent to synthesize nitrogen doped CDs (NCDs) from peach gum polysaccharide with an effective enhanced quantum yield (28.46%) compared to that of undoped CDs (5.31%). The high electronegative nitrogen atom gave blue shifted emission and induced an upper shift in Fermi level hence significantly enhanced the quantum yield of the CDs. Similarly, Hao Wang et al. [34] have synthesized NCDs via hydrothermal process (at 250 °C for 1 h) using urea as nitrogen precursor; it was reported that more amino groups conjugated on the surface of NCDs and this leads to enhanced PL characteristics. Nonetheless, the enhancement of optical property for the CDs derived from biomass via doping or surface passivation has not been properly verified. It remains unclear whether doping precursors could improve the PL and quantum yield of CDs from biomass which does not have well defined complex molecular structures.

Hence, this study aimed to understand the formation mechanism of CDs from biomass precursor by varying the synthesis duration and reaction medium as well as to investigate the influence of urea dopant on the quality of CDs produced. The CDs were synthesized from biowaste material – palm kernel shell (PKS) as carbon source. PKS is one of the abundant biomasses generated from palm oil industry, which is the largest industry in Malaysia. The CDs were produced via green and facile microwave irradiation technique. Previous study has indicated that PKS has been successfully synthesized to CDs [2]. However, the formation mechanism and stability of CDs have yet to be investigated. To further explore this aspect of study, the impacts of the synthesis conditions on the structural and optical characteristics of CDs were assessed to provide inputs for the understanding of CDs formation mechanism. Besides that, the synthesized CDs were utilized in cell imaging of Bacillus subtilis and Escherichia coli. Apart from that, on the basis of fluorescence quenching, the prepared CDs could serve as an effective sensor for determination of metals ions and also has been validated in real water samples. These CDs also demonstrated good characteristics to be applied as fluorescent ink and posed high photo stability in continuous UV radiation and various ionic strength solution; furthermore, the synthesized CDs exhibited pH dependent fluorescence behavior.

The CDs precursor – PKS was obtained from Tennamaram Estate, Selangor. Diethylene glycol (DEG) with purity > 99%, copper (II) sulfate solid and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Germany). Urea was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Malaysia). Stock solutions of metal ions including Al3+, Ca2+, Co2+, Fe2+, K+, Mg2+, Na+, Pb2+ and Zn2+ were obtained from ChemAR (Germany). Hydrochloric acid was purchased from J.T.Baker (Thailand). The real water sample was taken from the lake located in the campus. Aqueous solutions were prepared with ultrapure water (UPW: 18.2 MΩ cm, pH 7), which was purified using a Millipore Milli-Q system. The visual camera pictures were taken using Samsung A30 smartphone in bright lab conditions.

The fluorescent CDs were prepared by microwave irradiation using PKS as carbon source. First, 1.0 g of PKS powder was added into 30 mL of UPW plus addition of different urea concentration (0 mg/L to 0.04 mg/L). Then, the solution was sonicated in bath sonicator for 20 minutes to homogenize the mixture. The solution was then placed in microwave and radiated for 30 to 120 seconds using a Midea MM720CXM microwave at medium power (400 W). After radiation, the solution was naturally cooled at room temperature. Once completely cooled to room temperature, the solution was filtered using membrane filter (0.45 µm) and centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 20 minutes to remove any insoluble precipitates. The supernatant was then collected and filtered using microsyringe filter (0.2 µm). The synthesized CDs solution was stored at temperature of 4 °C for further analysis. All the synthesis steps were repeated by replacing UPW with 30 mL of DEG. The synthesis formulations are as shown in Table 1 for experiments on doping concentration and reaction time.

The quantum yield of CDs was calculated using the following equation:

where Φ and I are the results of the quantum yield and the intensity of the integrated radiation, respectively, and η and A are the refractive index and the optical density, respectively. R subscript refers to the fluorescence reference of known quantum results. In this case, the quinine sulfate (ΦR = 0.54) was dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4 (η = 1.33) while the CDs were dissolved in distilled water (η = 1.33) as preparation for the measurements. Absorption in a cuvette quartz 1 cm is maintained below 0.10 at 360 nm excitation wavelength to minimize reabsorption effect.

The synthesized CDs were characterized by various techniques. The microscopic images and sizes of CDs were obtained by transmission electron microscope (TEM) using Thermo Fisher- Talos 120C (USA). To identify the surface functional groups of CDs, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (Nicolet 6700, Germany) was used. Measurements for absorption spectra under UV radiation were obtained from UV–vis spectroscopy (Perkin Elmer- Lamda 35, UK). ZETA Sizer (Malvern Instruments Pvt. Ltd., UK) was used to measure the size and surface charges of CDs. The PL intensity of the synthesized CDs was recorded with single beam spectrofluorophotometer (Edinburg Instrument, United Kingdom) at room temperature. A PHI Quantera II X-ray photo-electron spectroscopy (XPS) system (Ulvac-PHI, Inc Japan) was used to investigate the functional groups present on the surface of the CDs with all the binding energies calibrated using C 1s as the reference energy (EC 1s = 284.6 eV). The fluorescence microscope (Carl Zeiss, Germany) was used in cell imaging application. Finally, the concentration of copper (Cu2+) ions was measured by an atomic adsorption spectrometer (AAS) using Perkin Elmer 800, USA. All pH measurements were made using digital pH-meter (827 PH LAB, Switzerland).

The synthesized CDs solution was used as ink to write ‘CD’ and ‘UKM’ on a round-shaped filter paper and left to dry for 30 minutes. Next, the fluorescence of the writing was observed under ultra violet radiation (UV 360 nm).

Pure strains of E. coli and Bacillus subtilis were obtained and cultured in nutrient agar using autoclave. Some of the cells were taken and incubated in 10 mL of nutrient broth for 24 hours at temperature of 37 °C. After the growth, cells from both pure strains were washed with phosphate buffered saline (PBS). Then, 150 μL of synthesized CDs solution was diluted in ultra pure water until reaching volume of 600 μL. About 400 μL of cultured cells were transferred into the diluted CDs solution. Next the CDs conjugated cell mixture was incubated at temperature of 37 °C for 1 hour. After complete incubation, the mixture was centrifuged at speed of 10000 rpm for 10 minutes to remove the supernatant. The obtained pellet is again washed with PBS solution and resuspended in PBS solution. Finally, the CDs conjugated bacterial cells were placed on the slide and observed under inverted fluorescence microscope. The fluorescence microscopy images were captured for both of the bacterial cells under different exciter filters which are bright field and fluorescence mode at excitation of 340–380 nm and emission of 435–485 nm [20].

Different metal ion stock solutions including Cu2+, Al3+, Ca2+, Co2+, Fe2+, K+, Mg2+, Na+, Pb2+ and Zn2+ were prepared. Then, 2 mL of CDs solution was added to each 2 mL of 0.2 mM ion stock solution and left to react for 10 minutes. All experiments were performed at room temperature. Next, different concentration of copper (II) sulphate solution were prepared (D1: 0 mM, D2: 0.1 mM, D3: 0.2 mM, D4: 0.3 mM, D5: 0.4 mM and D6: 0.5 mM). Then, 2 mL of CDs solution was added with 2 mL of copper (II) sulfate solution of different concentrations separately. The mixture was left to react for 10 minutes at room temperature. The sensing and quenching effectiveness of CDs in all the metal solutions were evaluated using UV–vis spectroscopy and PL spectrum. The performance of Cu2+ ion removal was determined using AAS and PL quenching. The removal efficiency of Cu2+ ion based on AAS can be expressed in the following equation:

[35]. Interference analysis was performed with the addition of 2 mL 0.2 mM of metal ion stock solution including Al3+, Ca2+, Co2+, Fe2+, K+, Mg2+, Na+, Pb2+ and Zn2+ separately in 2 mL of CDs and 2 mL 0.2 mM solution copper (II) sulfate and left to react for 10 minutes. Detection studies of copper ion in real water was studied using lake water samples. The water samples (ATK) spiked with copper (II) sulfate solution (E1: 0 mg/L, E2: 0.2 mg/L, E3: 0.4 mg/L, E4: 0.6 mg/L, E5: 0.8 mg/L and E6: 1.0 mg/L) were mixed with the synthesized CDs solution. The mixtures were allowed to react for 10 minutes at room temperature. The influence of the different metal ions in the presence of CDs and the real sampling performance were evaluated through UV–vis spectroscopy and PL spectrum upon excitation at 380 nm.

The pH of the CDs solution was adjusted to pH 3, 5, 9 and 11 using hydrochloric acid (HCl) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH). UV absorbance and fluorescence intensity of this sample were measured after 10 min to study the influence of ambient conditions on the fluorescent stability of CDs.

The effect of ionic strength (in terms of concentration of potassium chloride (KCl)) on the PL stability of CDs was investigated [7,16,36,37]. KCl solution was prepared in different concentrations of 0.2 M, 0.4 M, 0.6 M, 0.8 M and 1.0 M. Then, 5 mL of CDs solution was added into the KCl solution of different concentrations separately in centrifugal tubes. The mixture was left to react for 10 minutes and the salt resistance of the CDs sample were evaluated by UV–vis spectroscopy and PL spectrum upon excitation at 380 nm.

To verify the photo stability of the CDs produced using microwave irradiation method, the synthesized CDs solution was kept under continuous UV radiation for 180 minutes. Every 15 minutes, 2 mL of the sample was extracted to measure its UV absorption and PL intensity.

A detailed schematic of the synthesis of CDs is presented in Fig. A.1 (Appendix A). Several parameters such as doping concentration and microwave irradiation time might affect the optical properties of the CDs and the experimental studies were carried out with the purpose to obtain optimum reaction conditions for the microwave assisted synthesis. As for doping concentration, CDs without any dopant (sample B1) shows the highest fluorescent emission peak with intensity of 148,000 at 415 nm when it was excited at 380 nm (Fig. 1 (a)). The presence of hydroxyl and carbonyl group has contributed elements of oxygen to the surface of the CDs which helps in increasing the PL intensity of the CDs [38]. This can be evidenced by the presence of three types of functional groups containing oxygen such as stretches O–H at 3401 cm−1, CO at 1056 cm−1 and C–O–C at 1129 cm−1 on the FTIR spectrum (Fig. 2 ). The overall XPS spectrum of the CDs (B1 and B2) is depicted in Fig. 3 . The major peaks appearing at binding energy of 285 eV and 531 eV for both B1 and B2 samples indicate the presence of carbon and oxygen elements in the CDs [10]. Meanwhile, elemental nitrogen peak at around 400 eV clearly showed the presence of nitrogen in B2 (not observed in B1), which indicate the CDs are successfully functionalized with amine groups.

For samples B2, B3 and B4, the fluorescence intensity decreased drastically as the urea concentration increased. Contrary to previous studies [1], the doping did not enhance the fluorescent intensity, where the brightness of the CDs progressively decreased with the increasing of dopant concentration. The exact cause for this variance is unclear, but the working hypothesis is that it is due to the reaction of urea that is rich in N–H group with O–H groups in DEG forming some other molecules. The newly formed molecules prevented the formation of CDs because of limiting reaction media for complete synthesis to occur. A study conducted by Wang and his team found out that DEG can react with urea generated diethylene carbonate and NH3 [39]. Hence, it can be concluded that any increase in urea concentration will not suddenly increase the PL intensity of CDs.

Another postulation is that urea did not react well with biomass macromolecules, similar to earlier study where chitosan did not interact with PKS [2]. Past literature has shown that a substantial number of studies on the use of dopants in the synthesis of CDs have focused on simple organic carbon molecules. For instance, Lv et al. [40] have prepared NCDs with higher quantum yield (80%) via hydrothermal reaction (5 h at 250 °C) between citric acid as carbon source and ethylene diamine as nitrogen dopant. In another study, Qian et al. [41] have used carbon tetrachloride (carbon precursor) and butanediamine (nitrogen source) to synthesize NCDs with bright blue emission and enriched quantum yield of 36.3% via solvothermal method (2 h at 200 °C). The newly formed fluorescence centers via trapping of highly radiative electron-hole pairs, which is the real reason behind the luminescence enhancement via doping [5]. The doped heteroatoms become embedded in carbon structure of CDs, for example, nitrogen has 5 valence electrons on active atomic side for bonding with carbon atoms of CDs resulting higher energy levels, which leads to enriched PL intensity [3]. In this work, further experimental studies could be carried out to investigate the interaction between the reaction medium, carbon precursor, and dopant that might potentially affect the formation of CDs.

Moreover, a control experimental study was performed using UPW as reaction medium to support the study on influence of nitrogen doping on CDs synthesized from PKS. The microwave assisted synthesis of CDs from UPW under the same doping concentration range resulted in the formation of weak blue fluorescence emitting CDs (Appendices Section B). This might happen because the UPW with lower boiling point evaporated easily and PKS did not have sufficient time to interact with urea nor properly convert to CDs [42] while DEG with higher boiling point led to complete synthesis of CDs. Another possible reason could be that UPW lacked a carbonyl functional group and hence could not enhance the PL intensity, whereas DEG is a type of polyol solvent whose structure has more than two hydroxyl groups and multiple carbonyl groups [2,38]. The increased number of hydrophilic groups (carbonyl and hydroxyl group) from DEG were coated on surface of CDs, resulting in improved PL intensity, which is supported by PL spectra as shown in Fig. B.3. The detailed outcomes for control study are presented in Appendix B.

Among the samples, CDs-B1 had the highest quantum yield (43.07%), which is even higher than other previously reported CDs derived from biomass. For example, 22.31%, 16.8%, 24.87% 18.7% and 14.02% are the quantum yield values for CDs derived from coconut shell, orange peel, dove feather, sugarcane residue and egg shell, respectively [4,18,23,43,44]. These results prove that undoped CDs synthesized using DEG as reaction medium could serve as better luminescence probe for various sensing applications.

The measurement of UV–vis was also performed in an absorption wavelength of 200 nm to 500 nm at room temperature. UV–vis spectroscopy is an effective technique for analyzing the optical properties of CDs, and it is intertwined with PL spectroscopy. Referring to Fig. 1(b), sample synthesized without doping (B1) show two peaks, one at 280 nm, corresponding to the blue shifted π–π* transition of the aromatic sp2 domain [30] and another strong absorption band at 328 nm, usually found in energy-bound molecules with the transition n - π * bond CO [1]. It can be clearly seen that the peak intensity at 280 nm wavelength decreases with the increase of urea concentration. The electronic transitions (n - π * and π - π *) are illustrated in the hypothetical energy diagram (Fig. 4 ). The obtained results indicate the most preferred transition, which is from the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) to lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO). Bright fluorescence is detected when the electron is excited to higher energy levels (n–π* and π–π*) and returned to ground state by emitting a photon energy [25]. The results obtained from the UV–VIS spectrum are in good agreement with the outcome obtained by the PL spectrum.

To understand the formation mechanism of CDs, this study has optimized the time and results of the PL spectrum as shown in Fig. 5 (a). The fluorescence intensity increases gradually with the irradiation time from 30 s to 90 s but decreases when the irradiation time exceeds 90 s. Referring to the fluorescence spectrum, it can be observed that the C3 sample i.e. CDs synthesized at 90 s showed the highest peak of fluorescent emission with intensity of 190,000 at 415 nm when it was excited at 380 nm. Optimal reaction time increases the frequency of the formation of emission traps, arising from functional groups or changes in the hybridization of carbon cores and π domains on the CDs surface [45]. The highest PL intensity, obtained from C3 sample, may be due to the synergistic effect of both surface and carbogenic core conditions [24]. The intensity of the CDs synthesized at 120 s decreases because when the microwave irradiation time was too long, the surface structure of the CDs was destroyed, and the fluorescence intensity was reduced [46]. Moreover, the fluorescence of the samples aligned well with the quantum yield of the CDs synthesized at different microwave irradiation time, which were 27.21%, 33.42%, 43.07% and 31.96% for the C1, C2, C3 and C4 samples, respectively. Among the four samples, CDs-C3 had the highest quantum yield, proving that the CDs synthesized at the time of irradiation 90 s provides high fluorescence intensity in relation to the PL spectrum.

Based on the UV–VIS spectrum (Fig. 5(b)), it can be seen that the peak intensity at a wavelength of 280 nm increases gradually from the C1 sample and becomes the highest for the C3 sample. It can be concluded that the π–π* transition from the aromatic sp2 domain occurred and multiple CC functional groups were formed when the CDs was produced at 90 s of microwave irradiation [30]. This agrees well with the results of stretch vibration at 1651 cm−1 obtained in Fig. 6 (a). Any increase in synthesis time of more than 90 s, resulted in the destruction of the CC functional group on the surface of the CDs [47]. Careful observation reveals the formation of another weak absorption band at 325 nm in all four samples. This band was associated with the transition n–π* bond CO which is supported by the stretching vibration at 1056 cm−1 as shown in FTIR spectra [44]. It can be said that many CC functional groups are formed when the reaction time of 90 s was used for CDs synthesis. The results obtained from the UV–VIS spectrum are in good relation with the results obtained by the PL spectrum plus the results from FTIR spectra. The overall XPS spectra (Fig. 6 (b)) depict the two major peaks at 285 eV and 531 eV accords to the K-shell characteristics peak of C1 s and O1s, respectively [10]. The C/O ratio (Table C.1) increased with the irradiation time from 30 s to 90 s but decreased when the irradiation time exceeds 90 s. This indicates that more PKS were converted into CDs at reaction time of 90 s [48]. The high-resolution spectrum of C1 s (Fig. C.2) showed three different peaks at 283.8 eV, 285.2 eV and 288.1 eV, which corresponds to C–H, CC (sp2), and π - π * transitions, respectively [1]. Particularly, the C1 s peak at binding energy of 285.2 eV confirmed that the synthesized CDs were consisted of graphitic structure [10] and mostly constructed with sp2 carbon atoms [1]. From the XPS data, it can be concluded that the CDs derived from PKS have sp2 domain and carboxyl functional groups, which supported the results of FTIR spectroscopy and UV–VIS spectrum.

Apart from that, microscopic images and particle size were examined carefully with TEM. In Fig. 7 (a), micrographs of CDs with irradiation times of 30, 60, 90, and 120 seconds are shown for samples C1, C2, C2 and C4, respectively. At 30 seconds of the reaction time (Fig. 7 (C1)), no nanometer-sized particles were observed and only fragment-like structures could be seen. Various measurements of this structure were observed from 100 nm to as high as a few micrometers. In Fig. 7 (C2), micrograph captured after the microwave wave irradiation process for 60 seconds is shown. In this case, there are still ill-shaped particles measuring around 20–30 nm, which are much smaller than the previous sample but do not represent CDs indicating aggregation and unreacted PKS. Fig. 7 (C3) illustrates micrograph produced after 90 seconds of microwave irradiation where a clear transition to a nanometer-sized quasi-spherical CDs occurred, with an average particle size of 5 nm. At a reaction time of 90 s, the CDs has a uniform dispersion without aggregation. Finally, in Fig. 7 (C4), the TEM data for the CD after 120 seconds of synthesis is shown. In this case, the quasi-spherical particles of the CDs overlap each other and aggregation may occur due to excessive carbonation [30].

Referring to Fig. 7(b), samples C1 and C2 show size distributions between 20 and 80 nm while samples C3 and C4 show size distributions between 5 and 10 nm. The obtained size distribution supports TEM results as shown in Fig. 7(a). In addition, to obtain more information on the influence of the microwave irradiation time on the structural characteristics of the CDs sample, zeta potential analysis was used as it can provide detailed information on the size and charge of CDs. As for zeta potential (Table C.2), all samples C1, C2, C3 and C4 are negatively charged. The zeta potential is the crucial factor to determine the stability of colloidal dispersion. The obtained low zeta potential indicates that the CDs solutions are poorly electrically stabilized and flocculation tends to occur [49]. CDs produced at different reaction times show slightly negatively charged zeta potential, similar to previous studies when biomass was used as a carbon source for CDs synthesis, in which high-density functional groups were on the surface of CDs [18].

With this collective knowledge, a possible CDs formation mechanism from PKS via microwave irradiation was proposed. Initially, dehydration of PKS precursor occurs, resulting in the aggregation of decomposed PKS particles and mild condensation forming large nanoparticles (see Fig. 7 (C1)). These nanoparticles then shrunk in size due to continuous intramolecular dehydration during microwave irradiation [30]. At this stage, C–C bonds are formed, and at the same time, aromatic functional groups are formed. Once the concentrated clusters reach critical supersaturation point, nucleation of CDs occurs (see also Fig. 7 (C3)). At this stage, the nucleus is formed by the dispersion of aromatic groups on the surface of particles, and the passivation of various functional groups (hydroxyl, carbonyl, carboxyl, etc.) occurs [1]. Over time, nanoparticles tend to convert to CDs. In the final stage, small-sized CDs particles and narrower distributions are obtained. The proposed formation mechanism based on the TEM images aligned well with the reported mechanism by Papaioannou et al. [30] and Arul & Sethuraman [1]. In addition, the formation mechanism could be used to indicate the good quality of the CDs, and hence linked to quantum yield and fluorescent intensity, as the CDs with well organized arrangement of graphitic core will possess great luminescence property.

Based on the acquired outcomes, the synthesis time of 90 s and DEG without doping as the reaction medium were selected as the optimal parameters for further study of CDs application such as patterning, cell imaging, metal ion sensing and CDs stability.

CDs can be used as fluorescent inks due to their excellent optical properties as currently fluorescent inks are becoming popular for printing purposes and in the future can be used for information storage and data encryption purposes [50]. The urge to explore CDs as ink is due to the hazardous threats posed by the existing fluorescent inks towards nature. The existing fluorescent inks contain a wide range of heavy metals (for example, Cd, Pb and Cu), which are highly toxic to the environment [51]. Fig. 8 shows the writing of ‘CD’, ‘UKM’ and zig zag pattern written using CDs solution on filter paper under normal light (Fig. 8 (a)) and UV light irradiation (Fig. 8 (b)). These observations indicate that the distribution of CDs produced by PKS in aqueous systems has the potential to be used as eco-friendly anti-counterfeiting ink for further verification or concealment of important information.

CDs produced from PKS at the time of irradiation of 90 s and DEG as a reaction medium were incubated with E. Coli and Bacillus subtilis and cellular uptake were studied at room temperature, as shown by a photograph obtained by a fluorescent microscope (Fig. 9 ). Referring to the Fig. D.1, no fluorescent was observed from the control sample without CDs treatment. However, strong blue fluorescence was obtained when excited at 350 nm from the E. coli cell incubated with the CDs, indicating that the resulting CDs was successfully transported to the cell [4,52]. Similarly, blue fluorescent images were observed from Bacillus subtilis cells conjugated with CDs, when excited at 350 nm. Fluorescent microscopy images illustrate that E. coli and Bacillus subtilis can internalize with CDs dispersed in water. Furthermore, these pictures show the homogeneous distribution of CDs in both positive and negative gram cell bacteria. The images have revealed that CDs can successfully attach on the surface of bacterial cell. This mechanism can be attributed to the small-spherical CDs structure, 5 nm in size and having an hydroxyl group on the surface has contributed to the binding to bacterial cells [53]. The results reveal that the synthesized CDs shows good biocompatibility and can be applied in fluorescence cell imaging [12,54].

The CDs with the highest fluorescence is well utilized to study its potential use for metal ion sensing. This is motivated by the fact that CDs fluorescence are usually influenced by species such as cations when in close contact, which can then be modeled quantitatively to provide analytical information. In addition, there are several studies that have successfully demonstrated the use of CDs for sensing metal ions, although the CDs have been synthesized using different techniques and carbon sources [14].

Metal ions are commonly found in the environment mainly in water sources [18] and the metal ions selected for this study are Cu2+, Al3+, Ca2+, Co2+, Fe2+, K+, Mg2+, Na+, Pb2+ and Zn2+. As shown in Fig. 10 , these metal ions have different influences on fluorescence intensity of CDs. Metal ions can come in close contact with the CDs surface and cause different levels of disruption to the CDs fluorescence mechanism, depending on the correlation of their interaction and electronic configuration [23]. Based on Fig. 10, the most significant quenching was observed for Cu2+ and Fe2+ ions because Cu2+ and Fe2+ ions react with carboxylic functional groups and form coordination bonds, and as a result, CDs fluorescence is quenched by transferring electrons from functional groups on CDs surface to Cu2+ and Fe2+ ions. This result is supported by a study conducted by Tan et al. [14] where the fluorescence quenching of CDs produced from sago biomass can be seen with the addition of Cu2+ and Fe2+ ions. Thus, from these results, it can be concluded that the functional group such as carboxyl plays an important role in the optical properties of CDs. However, the exact cause for the increased fluorescence of other metal ions, namely Al3+ and Mg2+, is not known, but the working hypothesis is that it is due to the formation of some new bonds by the metal ions on the CDs surface and then forming new energy levels, leading to more fusion radiation between the holes and electrons. Thus, the PL intensity for CDs is increased [54].

Since, the decrease in CDs fluorescence intensity caused by Cu2+ is greater than that caused by Fe2+, further studies to analyze the PL intensity changes with different concentrations of Cu2+ ions are performed. The effectiveness of CDs fluorescence quenching increases gradually with an increase in the concentration of Cu2+ ions corresponding to the image of the solution under UV (Fig. 11 (a)). In Fig. 11(b), the ratio between the fluorescence intensity of the system before and after adding Cu2+ ions linearly decreased with Cu2+ ions concentration from 0 to 0.5 mM with a regression equation of F/F0 = −1.612C + 0.9574 (r2 = 0.9889), where F and F0 are the fluorescent intensities of CDs in the presence and absence of Cu2+ ions, and C is the concentration of Cu2+ ions. Strong electrostatic interaction between Cu2+ ions (positive charged) and CDs (negatively charged) causes the decline in PL intensity of the CDs [19]. Active adsorption site on cationic Cu2+ promotes adhesion of CDs, thus resulting in blocking of radioactive recombination of electron holes through an effective electron transfer process, and subsequently leading to quenching of fluorescent CDs [55]. Furthermore, the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated according to LOD = 3σ/S, where S is the slope of the regression equation and σ is the standard deviation of the y-intercept. In this case, the LOD for Cu2+ ions is about 0.05 mM.

The accuracy of Cu2+ ions removal percentage by CDs was benchmarked against the AAS analytical instrument. Fig. 11 (c) shows the removal efficiency of Cu2+ ions respect to initial concentration of Cu2+ ions, where the reduced concentration of same solution was determined using PL quenching and AAS technique. The removal percentage determined from AAS decreases from 81.2% to 71.8%, while PL quenching-based measurement shows a reduction from 78.5% to 66.8%, when Cu2+ ions initial concentration increases from 0.1 to 0.5 mM. The removal trend calculated from the reduction of Cu2+ ions concentration as measured by PL quenching was almost similar to that of AAS, demonstrating the PL quenching method has acceptable accuracy. The results suggest that the PL quenching of the synthesized CDs offer good potential to be used as measurement and removal of Cu2+ ions in aqueous solution.

Interference analysis was performed where the solution of Cu2+ ions was added to other metal ion solutions, namely Cu2+, Al3+, Ca2+, Co2+, Fe2+, K+, Mg2+, Na+, Pb2+ and Zn2+, separately. By physical observation (Fig. 12 ) it was found that the addition of Cu2+ ions in other metal ion solutions gave a quenching effect as no fluorescence was observed under UV radiation except for the control sample. Fig. 12 shows an obvious reduced PL intensity for Al3+, Mg2+ and Na+ ions when Cu2+ ions were added as quenching agent and similar results were obtained for the remaining metals (Fig. D.4(b)). Referring to the UV–VIS spectrum (a)), it can be clearly seen that control sample (CD) shows the highest absorption peak at a wavelength of around 280 nm associated with the π - π * transition from the aromatic sp2 domain occurs and the CC function group [3]. This confirms that the fluorescence quenching of Cu2+ ions was not affected even by other co-existed cations as shown in Fig. D.4(b)).

Given the acceptable selectivity (Fig. D.4(b)) and rapid reaction shown by the CDs produced by this microwave irradiation towards Cu2+ ions, the same application was tested against the actual water sample as shown in Fig. 13 . Prior to analysis, ATK samples were filtered using a 0.22 µm membrane filter to remove suspended particles. Then, Cu2+ ion solution at concentrations from 0 mg/L to 1.0 mg/L was spiked to the ATK sample. The blue fluorescence fades as the concentration of Cu2+ ions increased from 0 mg/L to 1.0 mg/L.

Moreover, ATK sample without addition of Cu2+ ions (E1) shows the highest PL intensity and no significant difference in PL result is observed when compared with control set with only UPW (E0). From UV absorption results in Fig. D.5, it can be seen that no absorption was observed except for ATK samples E1 having absorption peak at 280 nm, indicating the transition of electrons from phase π to π * and supporting PL spectra of CDs in ATK (Fig. 13). This proves that although there are many other elements in the ATK sample, the CDs remains relevant for sensing Cu2+ ions in ATK up to 1.0 mg/L. These observations meet the criteria of Standard A (0.2 mg/L) and Standard B (1.0 mg/L) set by the Department of Environment (DOE) for the content of Cu2+ ions in industrial effluent.

From previous section, we come to know that CDs are very advantageous in various applications. However, in order to synthesis CDs with good quality, a series of experimental studies has been conducted to explore the stability of the synthesized CDs in terms of pH, KCl concentration, UV exposure duration and reproducibility. The stability study will be beneficial since it is necessary for the prepared CDs to be utilized in many practical applications that involve different salt concentrations and also various pH solutions [16]. Also, an outstanding photostability of CDs are highly required for commercial purpose [56].

Studies to understand effect of change in pH towards the optical property of synthesized CDs were performed. Based on the PL spectrum (Fig. 14 (a)), it can be seen that the peak intensity increases gradually from pH 3 to pH 7 and decreases drastically when the pH value exceeds pH 7. Furthermore, under UV irradiation, bright blue fluorescence is observed when the pH value increases from 3 to 9, but at pH 11, green fluorescence is obtained. Such findings indicate an interesting phenomenon that is the pH-dependent PL property, indicating the potential use of CDs as a pH sensor [55]. The luminescence of CDs in solution of different pH values varied with the ionization states of either acidic or alkaline solutions [57]. The reduction of PL intensity of CDs in acidic solution can be caused by fluorescence quenching by hydrogen ions. Many particles aggregated due to the formation of hydrogen bonds with carboxyl groups on the surface of CDs. Also, at higher pH (pH > 7), decrease in luminescence is observed due to the increased level of deprotonation of chemical bonds on the CDs surface [58]. As a result, the carboxyl groups (COO−) on the CDs surface get the negative charge and would interfere with the fluorescence mechanism, which lead to PL quenching [59]. UV absorption and CDs PL (Fig. 14(a)) at pH 7 are the highest among the five pH values of 2, 5, 7, 9 and 13. Protonation of hydroxyl groups in acidic solutions (pH 3, pH 5) and deprotonation of COOH functional groups in alkaline solution (pH 9, pH 11) contribute to lower fluorescence intensity [27]. Thus, it can be said that PL decreases in solution of low or high pH, but remain constant at pH of 7 where the chemical structures of the CDs are less likely affected [45].

The influence of ionic strength on the PL intensity shown in Fig. 14(b). The results demonstrated that increasing the concentration of KCl did not cause any effect on the fluorescent intensity of the CDs. This is suggestive of the fact that the CDs solution is unlikely to undergo any salt interference and different ionic strength of KCl can assist in preventing the aggregation of the CDs as no precipitation is observed even upon addition of 1.0 M of KCl [37]. This indicates that CDs synthesized from PKS has great KCl resistance and can be used in biochemistry applications [60].

(Photostability of CDs plays an important role for good fluorescence properties. In this regard, a study on the effect of exposure time towards CDs fluorescence characteristics is carried out where CDs samples were stored under continuous UV radiation at 365 nm for 180 minutes. From Fig. D.10, it is found that all samples from the 15th minute to the 180th minute show maximum absorption at wavelength around 280 nm and the PL intensity maintains at about 95.8% after 180 minutes, which indicates good CDs photostability. Similar results were obtained in a study conducted by Xu et al. [55], where CDs fluorescence lasted for 120 minutes and even lasted after exposure under UV for more than 120 minutes.

To confirm the reproducibility of the method with regard to fluorescence properties of CDs, we studied the reproducibility of PL emission spectra of CDs by synthesizing CDs several times independently under the identical conditions. The results as shown in Fig. 14(c) demonstrated that the PL intensity peak for the four CDs sample was almost similar with the relative standard deviation (% RSD) value of 4.55%. Moreover, the CDs exhibit bright blue emission under UV light illuminations at 380 nm. These results indicate satisfactory reproducibility for the synthesis of biomass CDs.

The fluorescence lifetime indicates the length of time a molecule remains in an excited state before it reaches the ground state. The average fluorescence intensity lifetime is measured and calculated using fluorescence lifetime equation as shown below, where τ is the fluorescence lifetime and B is the indicative constant of relative concentration of each τ decay to the overall observed decay:

The outcomes are tabulated in Table S5-1. The calculated average fluorescence intensity lifetime is 3.18 ns. The obtained lifetime can be considered long (>3 ns) and this indicates a good emitting characteristic of the synthesized CDs [61]. Such typical lifetime of CDs may be explained by the localized conduction band electrons due to fast band gap transition, shallow trap states and oxygen related emission [26].

This study presents a simple strategy to synthesis CDs from easily available PKS using microwave irradiation technique. The influence of microwave irradiation time from 30 s to 120 s as well as the concentration of the doping agent on the CDs fluorescence properties were studied. TEM studies showed a decrease in particle size with increase in microwave irradiation time, and the highest level of monodispersion was achieved at synthesis duration of 90 s. Based on the above phenomenon, the mechanism of CDs formation is postulated, showing four stages including dehydration of PKS followed by polymerization, nucleation and growth. Further, FTIR and XPS spectroscopic studies revealed different bonds and possible different functional group bonds on the CDs surface. The UV absorption results proved the existence of different functional group bonds and the presence of different energy trap conditions on the CDs surface. The strong blue emissive CDs is pH dependent, has adequate salt resistance, good photostability under continuous UV radiation and reproducible. Due to its stable optical properties, the resulting CDs have the potential to be used as fluorescent ink and cell imaging probe for both gram negative and gram positive bacterial cells. The developed CDs sensor shows significant quenching only in the case of Cu2+ ions, which shows a good linear relationship (R2 = 0.9889) between the concentration range from 0.1 to 0.5 mM with a very low detection limit of 0.05 mM. Thus, this study demonstrated a successful strategy for sensing of Cu2+ ions in aqueous solution by employing synthesized green CDs as confirmed by the real-time test results.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The yellowish-brown solution was obtained after being irradiated in microwaves for 120 s at medium power (600 W), which confirmed the formation of CD as shown in Fig. A.1 .

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix B
ce:section.ce:para: a) Physical Observation

In this study, urea concentrations ranging from 0 g/mL to 0.04 g/mL were used as doping agents. Fig. B.1 (a) and Fig. B.2 (a) shows nitrogen doped CDs (N-CD) under normal light produced using UPW and DEG, respectively, and Fig. B.1(b) and Fig. B.2(b) show the N-CD under ultraviolet radiation.

From Fig. B.1, it was found that the color of N-CD solution darkens from yellowish brown to dark brown as the urea concentration increases, similar to the observation obtained in Fig. B.2(a). Furthermore, referring to Fig. B.1(b) and Fig. B.2(b), under ultraviolet irradiation, it was observed that the blue fluorescence fades as the urea concentration increases.
b) Optical property

Fig. B.3 shows the results of the PL spectrum for the CD produced by UPW. Referring to Fig. B.3, it can be observed that the sample A1, which is CD without doping, showed the highest fluorescent emission peak with an intensity of 50,000 at 415 nm when it is excited at 350 nm, showing good optical properties. However, a significant decrease in fluorescence intensity occurred when the urea concentration was increased for the purpose of doping as shown by the fluorescent spectrum of samples A2, A3 and A4. In addition, the quantum yield of CD at 350 nm excitation was calculated using quinine sulfate reference. The quantum yields of the CD synthesized by UPW were 26.31%, 8.87%, 7.03% and 6.97% for the A1, A2, A3 and A4 samples, respectively.

Fig. B.4 shows the UV absorption spectra of the sample set A (A1, A2, A3, A4). A strong absorption peak at a wavelength of about 245 nm along with the shoulder at a wavelength of about 280 nm can be seen in the four samples A1, A2, A3 and A4. The absorption peak at a wavelength of 245 nm is due to the electron transfer of the nano carbon structure, which is the π - n* shift of the O–H functional group conjugated from the carbon core (Papaioannou et al. 2019). It can clearly be seen that the peak intensity at the 245 nm wavelength increases gradually from the A1 sample and becomes the highest for the A3 sample.

In addition, careful observation indicate that there is another weak absorption band in the spectrum centered around 280 nm. The resulting shoulder may be due to the π–π * transition of group CC found on the CD surface. In fact, the absorption band is also a sign of the presence of different poly aromatic chromophores that appear during the production of all four CD samples from UPW at different urea concentrations (Sarkar et al., 2016).

Referring to the FTIR spectrum (Fig. B.5 ), sample A1 exhibits the absorption band of O–H stretching vibration at 3413 cm−1 and CO stretch vibration at 1727 cm−1, C–C stretching vibration at 1640 cm−1, C–H stretching vibration at 1471 cm−1 and the peak at 1151 cm−1 are attributed to C–O–C bending vibrations. Meanwhile, samples A2, A3 and A4, where N-CDs were produced using different urea concentrations, gave relatively similar FTIR spectrum results. Only three absorption bands can be seen, which are O–H stretch at 3393 cm−1, C–O stretch at 1645 cm−1 and N–H stretch at 710 cm−1.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix C
ce:section.ce:para: a) Physical Observation

Synthesis time ranging from 30 s to 120 s were used to study the influence of reaction time on the fluorescent properties of CD synthesized by DEG. Fig. C.1 (a) shows observation of CD (C1, C2, C3, and C4) under normal light and Fig. C.1(b) under ultraviolet irradiation. It was found that the color of the CD solution becomes more concentrated from light brown to dark brown as the irradiation time increases from 30 s to 120 s. Furthermore, under ultraviolet irradiation, it is observed that the blue fluorescence emitted as the irradiation time increases from 30 s to 120 s.
b) XPS Data
See Table C.1 and Fig. C.2 .
c) Size and Zeta Potential
See Table C.2 .

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix D
ce:section.ce:para: D1 Cell Imaging
See Fig. D.1 .
D2 Metal Ion Detection

Since, the decrease in CD fluorescence intensity caused by Cu2+ is greater than that caused by Fe2+, further studies to analyze the UV absorption changes with different concentrations of Cu2+ ions are performed as shown in Fig. D.3
. No absorption is shown at any wavelength when the concentration of Cu2+ ions increases from 0.1 mM to 0.5 Mm except a strong absorption peak can be seen at a wavelength of around 280 nm for the control sample (D1) (Fig. D.2).
D3 Interference Analysis and Real Sampling
See Fig. D.4(a) , Fig. D.4(b) and Fig. D.5 .
D5 Stability of CD
a. pH

Fig. D.6 shows the observation for sample CD in different pH solutions such as pH 3, pH 5, pH 7, pH 9 and pH 11 under (a) normal light and (b) ultraviolet irradiation. From Fig. D.6 (a), it is found that no change in color of CD solution is observed when the pH value increases from 3 to 9 and dark brown color is obtained at pH 11. Furthermore, under ultraviolet irradiation, bright blue fluorescence is observed when the pH value increases from 3 to 9, but at pH 11, green fluorescence is obtained.

Based on the UV–VIS spectrum (Fig. D.7 ), it can be seen that the peak intensity around 280 nm increases gradually from pH 3 to pH 7 and decreases drastically when the pH value exceeds pH 7. We can conclude that the π - π * transition from the aromatic sp2 domain occurs and the multiple CC functional groups are formed when the synthesized CD is in a solution of pH 7 which is neutral.
b. Ionic strenght

Fig. D.8 shows a physical observation of CD samples in KCl solution with different concentrations under normal light and (b) under ultraviolet radiation. It is found that the color of the six samples of CD solution remain unchanged as ionic strength increases. And under ultraviolet irradiation, it is observed that the bright blue fluorescence is almost unchanged with ionic strength.

Fig. D.9 shows the UV–vis absorption spectrum of CD solution added in different concentration of KCl solutions. The absorption peak at about 280 nm is associated with the transition n - π from CO bond. Meanwhile, the absorption peak at about 330 nm is associated with the transition of electron from surface level to π* level (Yoshinaga et al. 2019).
c. Exposure duration
See Fig. D.10 .
d. Fluorescence lifetime measurement
See Table D.1 .
